metadata.title: Adapt It + Justify It in Botspeak
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 BOTSPEAK PROMPT ADAPTATION — E3/5 — DOMAIN ADAPTATION (21 pts) + JUSTIFICATION (11 pts)
metadata.playlist: The Botspeak Prompt Adaptation — INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI
metadata.source: INFO 7375 (Computational Skepticism for AI) — Botspeak Prompt Adaptation (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Bonjour — this is Liam, in for Bear. Episode three of five: adapt the prompt and justify it in Botspeak. This episode covers two rubric components totaling thirty-two points — the domain adaptation at twenty-one, and the justification at eleven. The adaptation move is translation: every bracketed field in the original exercise prompt becomes role-specific, filtered through your spec. The justification move is causal explanation: you cite Loop steps, the Nine Capacities, or Specification components by name, and you explain why your role's delegation or discernment works the way you said it does — not just that it relates to those concepts. Name-drops lose points. Causal links earn them.

props.segment: Adapt It + Justify It in Botspeak
props.command: Translate every bracketed field using your E2 spec as the filter. Then justify each key choice with a causal Botspeak link — Loop, Nine Capacities, or Specification.
props.runningText: name-drops lose points — causal explanations earn them…
output: Adapted prompt — every bracket replaced with role-specific content
Justification — causal connection to Botspeak concepts, not just name-drops
Domain Adaptation (21 pts) + Justification (11 pts) complete

beats.act: the translation move — field by field
beats.narration_text: The adaptation is a translation. Take the original Botspeak exercise prompt, open it bracket by bracket, and for each field apply your spec from episode two as the filter. The spec tells you what the intent is, who the stakeholders are, what the constraints are, what must be excluded. Every answer to those questions is the content that fills the corresponding bracket. The test at each field is the same as the adjacent-profession test: could someone in a neighboring role write the same content into this field? If yes, tighten. 'A nurse' is still generic. 'A night-shift charge nurse who will cosign the discharge order in the next twenty minutes and bears institutional accountability if a contraindicated medication leaves the unit' is not.

shot.description: Horizontal flow diagram: three stages connected by arrows. Stage 1 — 'ORIGINAL EXERCISE' — a card showing a bracketed prompt with fields: [role], [task], [context], [stakeholders]. Stage 2 — 'SPEC FILTER (E2)' — a small card showing the five-component spec labels (Intent / Constraints / Criteria / Exclusions / Format) as the translation lens. Stage 3 — 'ADAPTED PROMPT' — a card showing the same structure with brackets replaced by specific content in terracotta ink. Arrow from Stage 1 through Stage 2 to Stage 3. Below the diagram, a 'FIELD TEST' question in italic serif: 'Could a neighbor write the same fill? If yes — tighten.' Claude palette. Fill the canvas.

beats.narration_text: Two ways to fail the adaptation. First: leaving a bracket in. Any unfilled placeholder in the submitted prompt is automatic evidence the adaptation is incomplete. Read your draft before you submit — if you see square brackets, you are not done. Second: filling a bracket with something so generic it could live in the original exercise. 'A healthcare stakeholder' is a generic fill. 'The attending physician who signed the original order and who the pharmacist will page if a contraindication is flagged' is not. The rubric is explicit on this: non-generic, role-specific content in every field. You earn the twenty-one points by demonstrating you actually know what happens in your domain — not by demonstrating you know what brackets are.

props.artifactTitle: Two Ways to Fail the Adaptation
props.artifactHeading: Both are automatic deductions — both are avoidable
artifactLines: FAIL 1 — Bracket left in: 'Review the [document] for [role-appropriate concerns].'
→ Read your draft. If you see [ ], you are not done.
FAIL 2 — Generic fill: '[role] = a healthcare stakeholder.'
→ 'A healthcare stakeholder' lives comfortably in the original exercise. Not adapted.
PASS: '[role] = the attending who signed the order; the pharmacist pages them if a contraindication flags.'
→ That content could only come from knowing this job.

beats.narration_text: The justification is eleven points and most students write it as a vocabulary exercise: 'This prompt uses Delegation because the user is delegating a task to the model.' That sentence name-drops a concept and explains nothing. What Botspeak is looking for is a causal explanation: why does delegation in this role look the way it does? For a night-shift pharmacist, delegation to the model is constrained by irreversibility — a discharge order cannot be un-issued once the patient leaves the unit, so the model's output is a flag, not a decision, and the pharmacist cannot hand off the final judgment regardless of model confidence. That is a justification. It cites Delegation from Chapter 4 and explains the causal mechanism specific to this role.

shot.description: Side-by-side contrast. Left panel: 'NAME-DROP (loses points)' — a quote in italic muted ink: 'This prompt uses Delegation because the user is delegating a task to the model.' Below: annotation 'Names the concept. Explains nothing.' Right panel: 'CAUSAL LINK (earns points)' — a quote in ink: 'Delegation is constrained by irreversibility: a discharge order cannot be un-issued. So the model's output is a flag, not a decision — the pharmacist cannot hand off final judgment regardless of model confidence.' Below: annotation 'Chapter 4 cited. Mechanism explained. Role-specific.' Terracotta accent on right panel header and the right-panel quote. Claude palette, full canvas.

beats.narration_text: The three domains of Botspeak vocabulary the justification can draw from: the Loop from Chapter 1 — predict, decide, verify — and why the Loop's three modes look different in your role's workflow; the Nine Capacities from Chapter 2 — the things the model can and cannot do, and which of those gaps your prompt closes or exposes; and the five Specification components from Chapter 3 — which component was hardest to specify in your domain and why, and what that tells you about where the model needs the most constraint. You do not need to cite all three. Cite the ones where you can make the causal argument. A shallow citation to three concepts is worth less than a deep causal argument about one.

props.artifactHeading: Cite to explain causally — not to demonstrate vocabulary
artifactLines: THE LOOP (Ch 1): predict / decide / verify — why does your role's workflow through the Loop look this way?
NINE CAPACITIES (Ch 2): which capacity gap does your prompt close — or expose?
SPECIFICATION (Ch 3): which component was hardest to write for your domain, and why?
→ You don't need all three. Pick the one where you can make a real causal argument.
→ Shallow citation to three concepts < deep causal argument about one.

beats.narration_text: The deliverable for this episode is two things: the adapted prompt with every bracket replaced, and the justification. Present them as labeled sections. The adapted prompt comes first — the full text, clean, no brackets. The justification comes second — cite the Botspeak concepts you are drawing from, then make the causal argument for why your adaptation choices follow from your role's actual constraints. The grader is reading for evidence that you understand your domain, not evidence that you read the textbook. Both are in the same document.

props.artifactHeading: Two labeled sections in one document
artifactLines: SECTION 1 — ADAPTED PROMPT: full text, no brackets, every field role-specific.
SECTION 2 — JUSTIFICATION: Botspeak concepts cited, causal argument made.
Grader is reading for: 'Does this person understand their domain?'
The causal argument is the evidence of domain understanding.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: 'I have a Botspeak exercise prompt with bracketed fields and a five-component specification. Here is the spec: [your spec from E2]. Here is the original exercise prompt: [original prompt with brackets]. Replace every bracketed field with content drawn from my spec — every fill must be specific enough that a neighbor in an adjacent profession could not write the same thing. Then, for each major adaptation choice, write one sentence explaining the causal Botspeak connection — cite the Loop, a Capacity, or a Specification component and explain the causal mechanism, not just the name.' Read the draft it produces. For any justification sentence that only names a concept without explaining mechanism, mark it and revise until the causal argument is there.

props.segment: Adapt the prompt using your spec — then justify each key choice causally
props.command: Replace every bracketed field using my spec. For each major choice, write one sentence explaining the causal Botspeak connection — Loop, Capacity, or Specification. Name the mechanism, not just the concept.
props.runningText: paste this into Claude — mark any justification that only names without explaining…
output: Adapted prompt — all brackets replaced, neighbor test applied
Justification — one causal sentence per major choice
Flagged: any sentence that names a concept without mechanism

beats.narration_text: Adapt It and Justify It in Botspeak. Liam, in for Bear.
